speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of the interview with Tola Hauptman. You were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Escape. Escaping. I forgot to tell you some small episode, which had a tremendous impact on me. That was when we were led to the-- on foot, the whole group-- and in the middle of the road, and the looks on people-- on the Polish people faces, and how I felt about it, that I'll never forget as long as I live.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a mixture with disgust. We were looking terrible. For instance, I was-- I was wearing boys' shoes, which were all hard and-- and broken, you know? And we looked outrageous. And the people-- I couldn't forget that. I was looking at the faces, and I thought, what would I give to be just a dog? Just a dog. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the one episode I wanted to mention. Now, the thing was, when I was running and went to the house where-- where I was-- that I knew that she lives. I was standing on the staircase, on the last floor, and I thought, I don't know what to do with myself. First of all, it's still light. I have to be here. But people are walking. They will ask me what I am doing here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this, were you at an address where your father sent you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Praga. No, no, no. That was next to the place where the Jews were working. On the [INAUDIBLE] side, that was Praga. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Wilenska [INAUDIBLE]. I can't recall-- Wilenska, I think. And all of a sudden, I-- I can hear the quick steps. And I thought, what if she would-- because I could feel that she likes me, that girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just saw a girl there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was in the kitchen with the caretaker. And she was doing some business with the Jews. You know, she was a young girl, only, but very [INAUDIBLE]. And I-- and it was her. And I said-- and she said, you know, I was thinking about you, whether you-- they didn't catch you. And I said, listen. You have to help me. You have to go with me to Grochow. I've got the address. I pay you because my parents gave me some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, oh, it's out of the question. My parents won't let me. You know? I won't-- I won't do it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] think about it. Is there a problem? So you took-- sorry about-- I'll just explain about the break. The scarf was falling on the microphone, so we had to have a quick break.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said to me, I go inside. And I-- I will feel the atmosphere. She went inside, and she said to her parents that she's going with her girlfriend. And she came back. And she took me there to Grochow.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't want any money. And also, I didn't take her to the place where I was going because then, I-- I knew my area, you see? So I thank her. We kiss each other. And I said, when I survive, I will see you, which I did. And then, I went to a place, another German name, Mr. [? Siedel, ?] who had a sort of a farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Warsaw. But he had the hot-- hot plants, you know, like under the-- like you see in the gardens, you know, under the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a market garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a market garden, yes. All  sorts of vegetables and flowers. And at-- at the time, he even wanted cheap labor, and he wanted my father to organize some [NON-ENGLISH] over there. But somehow, he didn't-- didn't come. You know? Anyway, that was the meeting place for my mother and me. It wasn't the-- the place where I should stay because, actually, what happened later-- that I found out that she didn't have a-- a steady place that I can stay. She only took me to some elderly couple. They were from Latvia, old people. And I was there two nights. In the meantime, she was trying to get me another place. And over there, it was very good. But they were so worried that their family will find out about me, you see, because she-- they would never agree. I was two nights over there, and my mother found a place in there-- there was a block of flats close to where I used to live. And it belongs, actually, to my uncle and auntie. And it was in one flat. It was a hairdresser-- an elder-- elderly couple. She was a hairdresser. He was a drunkard. But they didn't have any children. And how my mother found them, that's another story. I never found out, actually. But they were good to me. I was there. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three months. Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what date are we up to now, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, you see, the-- the thing is not that I am so old that I don't remember, which I am old enough. [LAUGHS] It can be an excuse. But when all of that finished, I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] decided to forget about it. And to forget, I was working on myself. I was trying hard to forget everything. And everything is, somehow, you know, the dates, especially, and the names, I forgot. So I can't tell you exactly when it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was about three months after you left the ghetto. When you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left that place, you were there for three months in that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about three months after you left the ghetto. When you were in that first place for any length of time, the place you were in for three months with that elderly couple--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the elderly--

speaker: interviewer
text: --how-- how did-- how did you occupy yourself? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was crocheting for the lady, for the woman. And she was so happy. But the neighbor of hers saw a beautiful jumper. As a matter of fact, my mother didn't have anything to-- but that was later-- to wear. So that I tell you later. But I was crocheting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was-- that was sort of my-- I was gifted in that way. And the neighbor asked her, look, I want a-- a jumper like you. Where did you buy it? She said, I've got a woman who is doing that for me. So she ordered one. And I made for her. But that neighbor was very [INAUDIBLE]. And she started to come over.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to tell you, it was-- that was workers' quarters, sort of-- one big room and the kitchen and a toilet. And-- and the kitchen wasn't separated, only it was a curtain in between. When someone rang, which it was very seldom because they didn't have friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I hide behind the curtain. And it was that case that she came. And she was so nosy. You know? She-- she even said, you know, I suspect that someone is in here. I saw that someone is living here. But I'm not sure. So-- so she said to-- to her no, look. Look for yourself. And she put the curtains on one side. And I was in the corner, behind the curtain.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, oh, I see I was mistaken. But she wasn't mistaken. And the next what I heard that was another couple, young couple, [INAUDIBLE]. So that means they were-- they were, you know, taking money if they found a Jew, they were taking whatever it was. And it was up to them. Either they send the person to the police, to Polish police, or they let them go. And I felt-- and my mother used to come every month and pay for me. That was it. I didn't have any money on me. And the last time when she came, these people told her-- the woman told her she can't stay any longer because the-- the next-door neighbor, she's suspecting. And it's dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother knew about it, but she didn't have any places. And I was desperate. I could feel that any time, the Germans will be there. And I remember as a young girl going to school-- because I-- I was already going to high school-- I had a friend who was very much developed.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was my age, mind you, only 14 or 13, but she was a beautiful girl from Bydgoszcz. That was close to the German, [INAUDIBLE]. And her father was a dentist. And they were, you know, well-to-do. And she had a little sister. And she was my best friend. She-- she was-- They were living there. And they sent her to her auntie where she used to live. But very often, she was coming to my place and staying over the weekend. And then we went together to school. She was my best friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was-- was she a Jewish girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stefa Blauzajd. Yes, it was a Jewish school. And she didn't look Jewish. And she was already flirtatious. And we-- did I say it right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to stay near to the motorist. And the motorist was a young Pole. Very good-looking. And she used to flirt with him. And I was laughing. And I was ugly. I had, at that time, I had a protruded teeth because that how-- how I was-- one centimeter-- I was-- I was wearing a special apparatus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Braces.

speaker: interviewee
text: Braces from gold and platinum to straighten my teeth. And I was very skinny. And my mother, on top of everything, she used to dress me as a little girl. And I hated it. They-- my friends, the ones who were well-developed, they were already going to the pictures, you know, seeing films, which were-- not allowed to. And I had to stay home. So that was bothering me. Anyway, she used to flirt with that motorist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Motorist? Someone who drove a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Drive a tram. We went by--

speaker: interviewer
text: A tram driver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tram driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he used to tell her all sorts of things. And also, he used to say that he's a leftist, he's a Communist. I remember that. And I knew where he lived. That was the next block of flats from where I was hidden. I remember that very well. And I thought, this is the only thing I can do. And I was reasoning with myself. Being a Communist, a young bloke, and a good one-- maybe he wouldn't turn me down. But I didn't know anything about his family or about his whereabouts. I didn't know a thing because he never talked to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went there one morning, early in the morning. And I knocked. And I was, as I said, I didn't have anything to wear. And I was looking dreadful. And he said, yes? I say, I want to see Lolek Slawinski. That was his name. I remember because I liked him as a young girl, I-- I really like him. And she said, he's not home. And maybe his family-- can I see someone? I'm his sister. And she was bitchy, terrible bitchy. But she-- she said, come in. I went into the kitchen, and met his mother, his younger brother, and her boyfriend who was about eight years younger than her or 10 years younger. He was a young boy. You know? And we're sitting all there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm talking. I don't know what got into me. The sister and the younger brother were very, very anti-Semitic. You see, very. If I say, 'very,' they didn't have an idea how a Jew looks. But it was ingrained in them, you know, the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, as you can you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. As you can sort of observe that I am Jewish. And I wanted to ask you if I can stay with you. My mother will come every month and pay for me. And she said to me, what are you, mad? She? Which one was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sister. The sister said, what are you, mad? We're going to hide a Jew? Oh, never. And anyway, who are you? I am-- my name is so and so. They heard about the name. And my father is very rich. When the war will end, you will be-- I mean, they will-- you won't regret. And she said, yes, but in the meantime, we will be all killed. That what she said to me. And I started to talk with them. I didn't have where to go. And anyway, was staying longer. And that Lolek came in. I said, I don't know whether you remember me. I used to go to school, and I used to meet you with Stefa. Oh, Stefa he remembers very well. Actually, he's got one-- because she was extremely good-looking, and she looked about 18 at the time, but she was only 14. And he said, I've got a-- another friend who works on the trams. And he knows her. She used to-- that behind my back because I never knew that she used to go out with him once or twice. And he said that he will hide her. I said, I haven't got a contact with her anymore. But you, oh, you better don't show yourself. He hates Jews, but he likes that Jewess. You know, that was his words. So I said, Mr. Lolek, I came about myself. I haven't got where to go. My mother is looking, in the meantime, for a place for me. But actually, I can't stay where I am. Maybe it's a question of months or-- so my mother will pay how much you want her to pay. And he said to me, I can't decide. This is my family's business. I can only say for myself. I'm not against it. I'm not for it. It depends for my family. And of course, the younger brother said no. And the sister said no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after I talked to Stasek who was a-- a boyfriend, he said-- he said to me, did you have a cousin Marek? Marek was his name. And next door, the flats belonged to them. And I said that was my first cousin, Marek Frydman. And he said, he was my best friend. And we used to have pigeons and all sorts of those [INAUDIBLE]. It's what do you eat-- the-- the white one. The-- you know, the white animal. We don't eat it, but they are very popular.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to look after them. He was very sort of like-- animals and birds and so on. So he had-- I could see that he's got sympathy already for me because I'm the first cousin. And-- and that Stasek said, look, you've got a radio. Because I said, it's a-- it's a penalty. It's a death penalty to hide a Jew. But they said-- he said, you've got a radio. And I said, so-- it's the same thing. For the radio, you've got a death penalty, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the death penalty was also when you were five minutes after police hour, which was 7 o'clock in the evening. You know, they were many death penalties. You see? And he started to work for me, sort of. I don't know why, but I-- I had a tremendous speech to them. I was-- I was never so outgoing. You know, I was rather shy. But I had a knife on my neck, you know? And he said, finally, all right, we'll see how we'll go. You see? And I was there-- I was there maybe eight months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. So you were there into 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: '43 Because I was there during the uprisings in the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I know? It was a red sort of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] red [INAUDIBLE]. Red-- the whole the whole sort of Warsaw was red from the fire-- how did I know. So they-- but I still didn't know what's going on. So they were very anti-Semitic, especially that-- Irena was her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she comes to me, and she said, come over, come over. Come to the window. And I said, Mrs. Irena, you know that I can't go to the window. You-- you told me yourself that I can't come close to the window. No, no. For a second. And I saw the-- the-- the red spots over the whole Warsaw. And she said, you see? You know what it is? I said, no. Your people are burning. She told me, your people in the ghetto are burning. So the only thing, I was desperate. She was really making my life incredibly hard. And I said, first, we are going. And then you are going. I don't know from-- I wasn't courageous. But I didn't want to leave. I didn't want to leave. And she took her coat. And she was running out. I'm going to the police.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Mrs. Irena, you're going to the police, but you know that I will tell them that I'm living here. When I said that, she took off her coat. And she didn't go. The other incident I had with her-- I was writing letters to my mother. I knew that it never will come to her hand. And even if I see her, I never show her. But I was-- instead of crying, I was writing those letters. And it-- it was a case that she found those letters. And she said, oh, no. It's in the end of you. And her-- that-- her brother, that Lolek Slawinski, said, if you go, I'll kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her oldest brother, who was a Communist. He said, if you go, I'll kill you. You know? And she didn't go. So she didn't go. Then she didn't give me to eat-- she didn't give me anything. I had-- her mother didn't have much to say. She was a-- he was-- she was good woman. She was a dressmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of a sudden, one day, I get up. It was very early. I can see some noise outside. And I look from far away. And I see the Germans every few steps. Germans staying around the-- the block with the guns. With-- not with the revolvers, with guns. And I thought-- and the younger brother said, oh, it's because of the Jewess! You know what you did to us! I was right! Now we all will die! You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- oh, he was furious, and his sister. I felt terrible. And what I knew that next door to us, I found out that it was another could-- young couple who took a Jewish people, young couple, also. That young couple had a daughter, little daughter. And they took a Jewish couple. And the woman was very attractive in a Jewish way. There is a film now, the which she probably will remind me. I didn't see the film yet. She was very attractive in a-- attractive in a Jewish way. And it was her husband-- very handsome. And they were living, and they were making parties as they were living officially like-- like Poles. And they were both, the Jew and the Pole, they were working for underground. And at that time because the Germans were there already, I tried to look over the window, and I can-- I saw the couple for the first time going out like anything, showing the papers that they are occupied somewhere-- they're going for work. And they went. That was-- I couldn't believe my eyes. But it had nothing to do with me. And I knew that anytime they will come and-- and found-- find me. You see, I had already Polish papers, which my mother arranged with the underground. I had to go personally to the Germans. And it was after a dead girl-- and the photo was changed into mine. And my signature was mine. You see, the signature was mine. I had to go there and put my signature in front of the Germans. I was trembling like that. And my mother was standing in the street, you know, because we couldn't be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you doing that at the same-- when you were still living in this house or before you came to this house? When did you get the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no. It was the-- it was the-- old people, the drunkard and the hairdresser.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right from the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, but before you go into that, we'll finish this tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. OK.
